what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard block item that makes a design argument about how much surrounding context should be captured when an annotation or comment is shared, proposing a GitHub-diff-hunk-shaped window and a split between minimal human-facing display and full machine-facing envelope data.
observations: The item is part of board://item-64466290 and was filed by ch-3mpkjg3r in the dragnet-development channel.
It cites Tim's instinct that responders need the annotated content or the annotation will not make sense.
It criticizes Medium highlights for under-capturing context by sharing only the highlighted run.
It treats GitHub PR review comments as a positive example because they ship the diff hunk plus surrounding context lines.
It warns against over-capture, noting Tim's complaint that the composer echoes selected content back on a screen where it is already highlighted.
It proposes the same envelope can have two renderings: minimal/elided for the human view and full structured context for the machine/agent view.
It references a brief section BD-C and a heading path / parent / siblings structure.
The file has no code symbols.
standouts: The GitHub diff hunk is used as the sizing analogy for annotation context windows.
The same envelope is proposed to carry full structured context while the UI shows only minimal/elided context.
The target node plus parent heading path plus immediately adjacent siblings is proposed as the right context shape.
conventions: Follows the channel-memory noticeboard YAML frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, thread, links, timestamps, and history.
Uses Markdown headings and bullet lists for the body.
Uses an informal, argumentative tone with emphasis via asterisks.
concerns: Several references (Tim, MSG2, brief BD-C, the composer, item-64466290) are not defined inside this file.
The claims are presented as assertions without supporting data or examples within the file.
There are no code symbols or concrete schema definitions for the proposed envelope.
notes: A design note arguing that annotation envelopes should carry a GitHub-hunk-sized context window and separate human display from machine payload.
questions: Who is Tim and what is MSG2?
What is brief BD-C and where is it defined?
What exact data structure is the 'envelope' and how is the context window extracted?
What does 'the composer' refer to in this system?
How are 'immediately adjacent siblings' determined for non-hierarchical content?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose in the title is to discuss which content and how much to capture when annotating; the body matches that purpose.
apparent_themes: Human versus machine rendering of the same data
Borrowing proven patterns from code review UX
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to shape a design decision about annotation envelopes by prescribing a specific context-window shape and by separating what the user sees from what the receiving agent receives.
novelty: It explicitly applies the GitHub diff-hunk concept to general annotation context capture and frames the same envelope as having two renderings rather than one.
contribution: It contributes a concrete sizing rule for captured annotation context (target node, parent heading path, adjacent siblings) and the principle that the envelope payload and the on-screen card can differ in richness.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note arguing that shared annotations should carry a GitHub-diff-hunk-shaped context window while showing only minimal context in the human-facing card.
intention_for_embedding: To influence the design of an annotation envelope by defining how much context to capture and how to render it differently for humans and machines.
